title: Usefulness of the Moral Law — Part I
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 05-21-23 - Usefulness of Moral Law (Part I) - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Review: The Moral Law and Its Uses (WLC Q. 94)
subpoints: The law cannot save — no one since the fall can attain righteousness by it (Romans 8:3)
Yet the law is still great and useful: useful to all men, the unregenerate, and the regenerate
The law is good if used lawfully (1 Timothy 1:8)
The three groups: all humanity, the unregenerate, the regenerate (WLC Q. 94-97)

outline.title: Guarding Against Legalism (Definition and Levels)
subpoints: Legalism is not merely having strict rules; it is something far more serious
Level 1: Making something other than the gospel the single identifier of Christianity
Jesus confronts this in Matthew 23 — tithing mint and anise while neglecting justice and mercy
Level 2: Seeking to earn God's forgiveness through personal obedience to God
New Testament examples: the rich young ruler, the Pharisee in the temple (Luke 18)

outline.title: The Spinning Plates Trap (Sinclair Ferguson)
subpoints: We tend to evaluate our relationship with God based on what we've done
Spiritual activities (Bible reading, prayer, church attendance) become "spinning plates"
When plates wobble, we feel distant from God — as if our status depends on performance
This is the error of mixing justification with sanctification

outline.title: Justification vs. Sanctification
subpoints: Justification: an act of God's free grace, declaring us righteous through faith alone in Christ alone
Romans 3:21-26: justified freely by his grace through the redemption in Christ
Justification is immediate, complete, and unchangeable — it admits no degrees
Sanctification is a continuing process — being made righteous in practice
We must never mix the two: our status before God depends on Christ, not our performance
Historical examples: Richard Baxter and John Bunyan both struggled with this distinction

summary:
In the second lesson in the Moral Law series, Pastor Tito Lyro takes up WLC Questions 94-97 on the usefulness of the moral law. He begins by reviewing the key premise: since the fall, no one can attain righteousness or eternal life by obeying the law — yet the law remains greatly useful. It is useful for all humanity, for the unregenerate, and for the regenerate, each in different ways. The lesson focuses especially on combating the error of legalism, which this section of the Larger Catechism is designed to guard against.

Legalism is carefully defined at two levels. At the first level, legalism makes something other than the gospel the single identifier of Christianity — a particular standard of dress, music, or practice that determines whether someone is truly a believer. Jesus confronts this in Matthew 23, pronouncing woe on those who tithe mint and anise while neglecting justice, mercy, and faith. At the second and more pervasive level, legalism is seeking to earn God's forgiveness through personal performance. The rich young ruler and the Pharisee praying in the temple are New Testament illustrations. Most believers, Lyro argues using Sinclair Ferguson's illustration, struggle with this second form: treating their spiritual disciplines as spinning plates whose continued motion determines their acceptance before God.

The lesson then carefully distinguishes justification from sanctification. Justification is a single, complete, unrepeatable act of God's free grace at conversion — it admits no degrees and cannot be improved or diminished. Sanctification is the ongoing, progressive work of grace making us more like Christ in practice. Confusing the two — treating our sanctification as contributing to our justification — is a practical form of legalism that even godly men like Richard Baxter and John Bunyan struggled with at points.
transcript:
## Introduction

When we dismissed our Sunday school classes to the kids, I'm confident that there are some people that just get up, go for a lap and come back in, because I don't understand why they would have a reason to get up and go somewhere. Whatever it takes for you to stay awake and pay attention, that's great.

We started last week on the moral law of God and we continue that this week. And today we're going to start looking at the usefulness of the moral law. The question is questions 94 through 97. This will take two weeks for us to go through this lesson.

You may have heard someone talk about the three uses of the moral law. It's a reformed way of talking about the purpose that the moral law has. You may have heard it being talked about the pedagogical use — the law acts as a schoolmaster to drive us to Christ. You may have heard of the civil use — the moral law serves as restraint among society, so that mankind doesn't get as bad as it can get. And then there's a third use, often the normative use — to tell us what is pleasing to God and what we should do. Who has heard of these things before? Would you mind raising your hand? Okay. That's not what we're talking about today. The Larger Catechism, when it talks about the uses of the law, is not talking about this sort of scenario. This is true and right and helpful, but that's not what the Larger Catechism is talking about when it talks about the uses of the law.

For today and then two weeks from today we're going to be considering the moral law and its usefulness — what is it useful for? And the Larger Catechism says it's used for three groups of people. It's useful for all humanity, it's useful for the unregenerate, and then it's useful for the regenerate. And there are different ways the moral law interacts with these different groups of people.

Last week we took a look at just what the moral law is, question 93: "The moral law is the declaration of the will of God to mankind, directing and binding everyone to personal, perfect, and perpetual conformity and obedience thereunto in the frame and disposition of the whole man, soul and body, and in performance of all those duties of holiness and righteousness which he owes to God and men, promising life unto the fulfilling and threatening death unto the breach of it."

That's what the moral law is. It's interesting that when the confession or the assembly approved these catechisms, in the preface to the Larger Catechism it said this is for the church in general, and then when the preface to the Shorter Catechism said this is for children and those not intelligent enough to memorize the Larger Catechism. And today we are doing well if we can even think about what the Shorter Catechism says, let alone the Larger Catechism. So one of the reasons for this lesson is because we do need to be, I think the moral law has been deemphasized in our reformed world today. But also to help us become more familiar with how helpful the Larger Catechism is.

So the moral law is the will of God revealed to us — what God requires of us, what we are to believe concerning God.

## WLC Question 94: The Law's Use Despite Its Inability to Save

Alright so today we're going to take a look at really just one question, but we'll read all of them. Question 94 asks: "Is there any use of the moral law to men since the fall?" Although no man since the fall can attain to righteousness and life by the moral law — and that's really what we're going to spend our time on today — "yet there is great use thereof, as well as coming into all men, as peculiar either to the unregenerate or the regenerate."

So in a nutshell: yes, the moral law is still good, still useful, even though you cannot earn your way to heaven or fellowship with God through obeying it. There is great use for it among God's people, but also in humanity in general. You can see all three groups listed there — to all men, to the unregenerate, and to the regenerate — and it functions in different ways to each one of these groups.

The Bible teaches that in Romans 8:3 — since the fall, no man can attain righteousness and life by personal obedience to the moral law: "For what the law could not do in that it was weak through the flesh, God did by sending his own son in the likeness of sinful flesh on account of sin; he condemned sin in the flesh."

Galatians also teaches us the same thing as Romans 8:3 where Paul says, "knowing that a man is not justified by the works of the law but by faith in Jesus Christ, even we have believed in Christ Jesus that we might be justified by faith in Christ and not by the works of the law, for by the works of the law no flesh shall be justified."

But the question also says that the law is useful even though you can't attain fellowship with God and eternal life through the keeping of it. It is still useful. 1 Timothy 1:8: Paul says we know that the law is good if one uses it lawfully. So there's a good way to use the law, and the law of God is still valid and good for us today.

This particular question is guarding against the popular error concerning the moral law that says that humanity can save itself by good works or by good character. Now unregenerate people — people who have not been born again — cannot keep the moral law at all so as to please God. That is an impossibility. Romans 8:8 says that natural man cannot please God. But it goes beyond that: even their good works — what in a horizontal plane might be considered good works — are sins that need to be repented of. In Proverbs 21:4 it says that the plowing of the wicked is sin — so even the good activity of plowing the land to produce food, by the unregenerate, is also a sinful action in regard to God.

True believers in Christ, by divine grace, are unable to keep the moral law of God even perfectly, and the good works are acceptable to God only because of Jesus' mediation — because Christ mediates our works for them. It's like we do good works here by God's grace in faith in Christ, Christ grabs them, actually perfects them, and then presents them to the Father. So the believer, the regenerate, can obey the law of God, but even that obedience imperfect; if it wasn't for Jesus Christ presenting those good works before the Father, they would not be acceptable.

## What Is the Law Useful For? (WLC Q. 95-97)

If not to attain righteousness, what is the moral law useful for? Let me read through questions 95 through 97 aloud while you read silently.

Question 95 asks: "What use is the moral law to all men?" The moral law is of use to all men to inform them of the holy nature and will of God and of their duty binding them to walk accordingly; to convince them of their disability to keep it and of the sinful pollution of their nature, hearts and lives; to humble them in the sense of their sin and misery and thereby help them to a clearer sight of the need they have of Christ and of the perfection of his obedience.

A question that might come to your mind: it says the moral law is useful to all men, but there are people in the world that haven't heard the Bible. The answer would be: the moral law is written in every person's heart. So there's no one who has ever lived, who lives, and will live who does not have the moral law of God available to them — it lives in their hearts.

Question 96 asks: "What particular use is there of the moral law to the unregenerate man?" The moral law is of use to unregenerate men to awaken their conscience to flee from the wrath of God to come and to drive them to Christ; or upon their continuance in the state and way of sin, to leave them inexcusable and under the curse thereof. So the moral law to the unregenerate either drives them to Christ or condemns them.

Question 97 asks: "What special use is there of the moral law to the regenerate?" Although they that are regenerate and believing in Christ be delivered from the moral law as a covenant of works so as thereby they are neither justified nor condemned, yet besides the general uses thereof common to them with all men, it is of special use to show them how much they are bound to Christ for his fulfilling it and enduring the curse thereof in their stead, and for their good, and thereby to provoke them to more thankfulness and to express the same in their greater care to conform themselves thereunto as the rule of their obedience.

Do you see the amount of grace that's here in this question? The moral law helps us see how much we need Christ as believers. And out of thankfulness for that realization — when we recognize that we can't really do the things that God calls us to do in a consistent basis, and that he still has fellowship with us, he still accepts us — then out of thankfulness we're encouraged to obey more the moral law of God.

## Legalism: Two Levels

Now this is just a very brief overview for next week, but what we want to do is look at the idea that this section particularly, question 94, combats the idea labeled in history as legalism, which is a common tendency that we all have. So that's what we're going to spend the rest of time talking about.

Legalism is not just a matter of someone who has higher standards or more rules than you have. That's not what legalism is. Now we often wrongly label a legalistic person as someone who never goes to movies, or who thinks that any music with a beat is from the devil. Legalism is much, much more subtle and serious than that. We also wrongly label as a legalist those who have standards that we don't like, even though those standards might be biblical.

Let me define legalism in two ways.

Legalism is making something that is not the gospel the single identifier of Christianity. What I mean by that is: if you do this certain thing, you are a Christian; if you don't, you're not. Or if you don't do this certain thing, you're a Christian; if you do, you're not. And things that come up may even be important, but they're not the gospel itself. We find an example of Jesus confronting that in Matthew 23: "Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! For you pay tithe of mint and anise and cumin and have neglected the weightier matters of the law: justice and mercy and faith. These you ought to have done without leaving the others undone. Blind guides, who strain out a gnat and swallow a camel!" Notice what Jesus is doing here — he's pronouncing a curse. And why? Because they are making things that are important in a peripheral way to be the core of the gospel. "If you don't tithe the way we do then you're not a follower of the God of the Bible." Note though that he doesn't say they should not have done the specific tithing — but that they should have done it in the proper perspective, not denying justice and mercy and faith.

The second level of legalism is far more prevalent than the first, actually, and that is seeking to achieve forgiveness from God and justification before God through obedience to God. A legalist in this second sense is anyone who behaves as if they can earn God's forgiveness through personal performance. New Testament examples: the rich young ruler in Mark 10 — "Good teacher, what shall I do that I may inherit eternal life?" And look at the commandments he lists: good things, but not designed to secure God's forgiveness. And the Pharisee praying in Luke 18: "God, I thank you that I'm not like other men — extortioners, unjust, adulterers, or even as this tax collector. I fast twice a week; I give tithes of all that I possess." He was banking on his righteousness for God's approval. And Jesus says of the tax collector, "This man went down to his house justified rather than the other."

## The Spinning Plates Trap

At this point you might say, I don't struggle with this problem. But if you're in that place, listen to what Sinclair Ferguson says: "How easily we fall into the trap of assuming that we remain justified only so long as there are grounds in our character for justification. We tend to evaluate our relationship with God based on what we've done."

He says we tend to liken our life with God to the spinning of plates. You keep spinning the plates — your Bible reading, your prayer, your church attendance, all these good and righteous things — but when a plate wobbles, or when one crashes, oh no, your worth before God is gone. And these spiritual activities become simply more spinning plates to maintain. Then when Sunday morning comes, you'll sing and praise God with evidence and serenity and zeal when you've had a really good week with not even a single plate wobbling. But on another Sunday following a week in which several plates crashed, you are hesitant to approach God and find it difficult to worship freely. In that case, you are saying that your status before God is dependent on what you've done that week, not upon Jesus Christ.

## Justification vs. Sanctification

Theologically this often happens because we mix two separate doctrines: we mix the doctrine of justification with the doctrine of sanctification. Breaking free from legalism doesn't mean that you stop reading your Bible, or that you stop praying, or that you stop sharing the gospel. It's about where you're putting your trust.

In the moment you repented from your sins and trusted in Jesus Christ, you were justified. You were declared righteous before God, and that cannot be changed. Romans 3:21-26: "Now the righteousness of God apart from the law is revealed, being witnessed by the law and the prophets, even the righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ to all and on all who believe. For there is no difference, for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, being justified freely by his grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus." It's not through our prayer, not through our Bible reading, not through our sharing the gospel, not through our keeping up with the Joneses — it's through Jesus Christ, and it's free.

Justification refers to our status before God. When we put our faith in Jesus Christ, God the Father hands down the verdict that you are righteous, and there's nothing that will change that. He transfers the perfect, sinless record of Jesus to us and our sins to Jesus — that transaction is called imputation. The Shorter Catechism: "What is justification? Justification is an act of God's free grace, wherein he pardons all our sins and accepts us as righteous in his sight, only for the righteousness of Christ imputed to us, and received by faith alone."

As William Plumer, a Scottish commentator writing in the early to mid 1800s, says: "Since justification is an act, it's not a work or a series of acts. It's not progressive. The weakest believer and the strongest saint are alike equally justified. Justification admits no degrees. A man is either wholly justified or wholly condemned in the sight of God." You're either justified or you're not. You're not kind of justified. Not in and out. Either you are or you are not.

And the legalist confuses their own ongoing participation in the process of sanctification with God's finished work in justification. They tend to put their sanctification before their justification. When I do all these things, when I am sanctified enough, then I will arrive at my status before God. They assume that godly practices and good works somehow contribute to their justification. None of our good spiritual activities add to our justification. We're never more saved or more loved by God.

Sanctification, on the other hand, is a work of God's free grace that continues in our lives. Shorter Catechism Q. 35: "What is sanctification? Sanctification is a work of God's free grace, whereby we are renewed in the whole man after the image of God and are enabled more and more to die unto sin and live unto righteousness." That's something that continues on in our lives but doesn't change our status before God.

Justification is our position before God — a position that becomes permanently ours at the time of our conversion. Sanctification is our practice that continues throughout our lives. Justification is immediate and complete upon conversion. Sanctification is a process that continues. Justification is objective — Christ works for us. Sanctification is subjective — Christ works in us.

Even godly men have struggled with that. Two that you need to be aware of: one is Richard Baxter. Richard Baxter really struggled with this idea that it's by faith alone that you're justified before God and that your sanctification doesn't really play a factor in that. The other one is John Bunyan. You should all read Pilgrim's Progress — it's a great book. But remember: when does Christian lose his burden? It's well into his walk as a Christian. He's already come to faith in Christ and yet he's still carrying his burden, almost as if he's atoning for it. But where the burden drops is at the cross — at the moment of faith. So you can see that this is a struggle that humanity has. We sadly prefer being our own savior than just embracing Christ totally as our Savior.

So that's what this section of the Larger Catechism is combating: that we cannot have fellowship with God by simply keeping the moral law. We cannot obtain eternal life by keeping the moral law. And now that we've looked at this, now we're ready to study the moral law and its uses, starting two weeks from today, because next week we have the last "Trusting God" study.

All right, so let's pray. Father in heaven, thank you that you are a good God. We thank you for your word that so clearly proclaims Christ as the Justifier. We thank you that you are just and the Justifier. Help us to be satisfied with that. Pray that you keep us from trying to add anything to our faith in Christ. In his name, amen.
